mnemonics.title: AAA - Association, Aggregation, Abstraction
mnemonics.content: Remember: Association is general (line), Aggregation has diamond (empty), Composition has diamond (filled). Association Aggregation Abstraction - Always AAA in class diagrams

mnemonics.title: C-O-N-S - Class Object Name Super
mnemonics.content: For CRC Cards: C - Class name, O - Object responsibilities, N - Name the collaborators, S - Superclass relationships

mnemonics.title: P-A-K-E - Public + Private Protected # Package ~
mnemonics.content: Visibility symbols: P = Public (+), A = Private (-), K = Protected (#), E = Empty/Package (~). Think 'P-A-K-E' sounds like 'cake'

mnemonics.title: GADA - Generalization Dependency Aggregation Association
mnemonics.content: Relationship strength order (strongest to weakest): Generalization (strongest) → Dependency (weakest). Remember: 'Gently Deliver Apples And' (GADA)

acronyms.usage: Index cards used to identify and organize object-oriented classes during analysis
Standard language for visualizing, specifying, constructing, and documenting software systems
Methodology for analyzing and designing a system using objects/classes
Alternative to class diagrams for data-centric modeling

acronyms.content: Classic problem: Place N queens on N×N chessboard with no attacks

visualTips.visualization: Draw an empty diamond for aggregation (has-a, can exist alone) and a filled black diamond for composition (contains, dies together). Example: University (filled diamond) contains Departments vs Department (empty diamond) has Professors
Use a number line visualization: 0..1 = optional (0 or 1), 0..* = zero to many (unbounded), 1..* = one to many (at least one), n = exactly n. Draw these on both ends of association lines

Think of a three-story building: Name on top floor (bold), Attributes in middle (data), Operations on bottom floor (actions). Use traffic light colors: + green (public), - red (private), # yellow (protected)
Draw an upside-down tree. Superclass at top (root), subclasses below (leaves). Arrow points from subclass TO superclass. Label with triangle arrowhead pointing to parent

analogies.analogy: A class is like an architectural blueprint for a house. The blueprint defines the structure (attributes) and functionality (rooms, plumbing) but is not the actual house itself. Multiple houses (objects) can be built from one blueprint.

Aggregation is like a library having magazines. If the library closes, magazines could be moved to another library - they exist independently. Composition is like a book having pages - tear out the pages and you don't have a book anymore.

Think of a company organization chart. Each department (class) has responsibilities ('Finance handles money') and collaborators ('HR works with Finance for payroll'). CRC cards formalize this for software.

Dependency is like a customer who visits a shop. The customer 'uses' the shop but doesn't own it. If the shop closes, the customer is affected but continues to exist. The customer knows about the shop (dependency arrow points to shop).
